title: Portfolio Management and Investment Advice

narrative.executive_view: A wealth-management RIA in the $1-10 million EBITDA band offers a large AI-addressable task pool, but current acquisition pricing leaves little safe multiple arbitrage. Planning drafts, meeting preparation, research synthesis, portfolio monitoring, account operations, billing, client-service workflows, and compliance documentation are tractable; client acquisition, behavioral coaching, fiduciary accountability, and trust remain human-intensive. Schwab found 68% of surveyed RIAs using AI in some way [S1], so the opportunity is no longer speculative, but effective labor substitution is behind tool use. AUM-based fees decouple price from hours and support retention of savings, while documented fee compression and easy access to automated advice limit capture. Paid advice is pressured rather than melting: the market is growing and regulated, but mature conversational AI is a more credible substitute for mass-affluent service than the prior robo-adviser wave.
narrative.how_ai_changes_work: The operating model can shift from people assembling information to people supervising, interpreting, and communicating it. Advisors can automate plan drafts, meeting briefs and notes, proposal generation, and routine follow-up; analysts can automate screening, research summaries, rebalancing, and performance attribution; operations teams can automate account opening, transfers, billing, reconciliation, RMD workflows, scheduling, and CRM hygiene. The SEC's robo-adviser guidance confirms that algorithmic advice can generate and manage portfolios with limited human interaction, while remaining subject to disclosure, suitability, compliance, and fiduciary obligations [S13]. Those obligations preserve review and accountable supervision, but they do not preserve every current task or headcount position.
narrative.value_capture: Distribution retention is structurally favorable because the dominant price is a percentage of assets, not hours worked: 95.5% of SEC-registered advisers offered AUM-based fees in 2024, although only 17.4% relied on that model alone [S11]. Labor savings therefore do not automatically reprice the client contract. Capture is incomplete. Cerulli reports fee pressure, broader service expectations, and an expected average fee around 66 basis points for clients above $10 million [S10]. Independent books are also portable; Cerulli observed asset losses even in adviser transitions between independent firms [S9]. Institutionalized service teams, next-generation equity, niches, and strong client retention can protect savings, while a founder-centric book or commoditized allocation proposition cannot.
narrative.adoption_timing: Schwab's 2025 benchmark reports 68% of 1,288 custody clients using AI in some way [S1]. That headline includes experiments and low-intensity uses, so the packet estimates only 40% current effective adoption of economically material workflows and a two-year path to 50%. The speed case is strong because meeting tools, custodial APIs, CRM assistants, and research copilots fit existing software. The slower case is driven by privacy, recordkeeping, hallucination risk, integration, and the need for supervised advice. Adoption can reach a majority before the full labor-saving fraction is realized.
narrative.consolidation_economics: The RIA market is exceptionally active. ECHELON counted 466 wealth-management transactions in 2025 and listed eight strategic RIAs with ten or more acquisitions, led by Wealth Enhancement at 20 and Mercer Advisors at 17 [S8]. Current multiple evidence does not support a cheap-entry story: Advisor Growth Strategies' 2026 report, summarized from 60 actual 2025 transactions, places the median at 11.6x adjusted EBITDA and the average seller around $500 million AUM [S7]. The prior report already showed an 11.0x 2024 median, included sub-$2 million-revenue transactions, and emphasized earnouts and contingent consideration [S6]. The packet therefore uses 11.6x entry and only 13.0x exit; the latter assumes scale and institutionalization but stays far below 20x-plus large-platform headlines. Any real return must come primarily from organic growth and operating improvement, not assumed multiple expansion.
narrative.terminal_demand: Paid demand is pressured. Industry scale and participation continue to grow: IAA reports 16,544 SEC-registered advisers, 73.7 million clients, and record assets in 2025 [S2]. Federal law imposes a fiduciary duty across the adviser-client relationship [S12], and complex tax, estate, behavioral, family, and concentrated-asset decisions benefit from accountable judgment. Yet the SEC has long recognized fully or partly automated advisers under the same regulatory framework [S13], so regulation is not a human-only moat. Basic portfolio construction and routine planning can migrate to cheaper AI-native providers; durable demand is strongest for complex households and firms that sell coordination, judgment, and accountability rather than information access.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: NAICS 523940 combines wealth advice with portfolio and institutional asset management, whose fee rates, staffing, margins, and automation differ sharply. The deterministic labor share is ancestor-level and the role mix is three-digit 523, including brokerage sales agents and larger-manager software staff. The fallback target count is a deliberately rough AUM-to-revenue-to-margin bridge and excludes state advisers that are mostly below the inferred EBITDA threshold. Transaction multiples are adjusted EBITDA headline values with meaningful equity, earnout, and retention components; owner compensation normalization can move the denominator. Market declines lower AUM fees immediately, founder departures can trigger asset loss, and AI could increase service expectations rather than reduce headcount.

sources.title: 2025 RIA Benchmarking Study
sources.what_it_supports: Survey of 1,288 Schwab-custodied RIAs representing more than $2.4 trillion AUM; 68% reported using AI in some way and the page states sampling/self-report limitations.

sources.title: Investment Adviser Association — Industry Statistics and 2026 Snapshot
sources.what_it_supports: 2025 Form ADV industry counts: 16,544 SEC advisers; 67.4% under $1B AUM, 87.3% under $5B; advisers focused on individuals averaged eight employees and $424M AUM.

sources.title: 2019 RIA Benchmarking Study — Firms under $100M and Peer Groups
sources.what_it_supports: Historical Schwab peer margins: standardized median operating margins around 24.2%-30.0% across AUM groups, including 29.9% for $500M-$750M firms.

sources.title: Overpaying for Financial Advice? A Guide to Fees
sources.what_it_supports: Secondary fee benchmark of 0.88% on a $3 million client account and discussion of AUM versus flat-fee structures.

sources.title: NASAA 2025 Investment Adviser Section Report
sources.what_it_supports: State-regulated population outside the SEC snapshot: 16,575 home-state investment advisers and approximately $380.3B total AUM as of year-end 2024.

sources.title: The RIA Deal Room 2025 — Navigating the High Tide
sources.what_it_supports: Actual-transaction methodology, 11.0x 2024 median adjusted EBITDA multiple, seller revenue distribution, contingent consideration, size/quality effects, and large-platform headline caution.

sources.title: RIA Valuations Hit New Record in 2025
sources.what_it_supports: Advisor Growth Strategies 2026 Deal Room findings: 60 transactions, 11.6x median adjusted EBITDA, 276 deals, and average seller near $500M AUM.

sources.title: ECHELON 2025 RIA M&A Deal Report
sources.what_it_supports: 2025 wealth-management deal volume, buyer-type activity, seller-size trends, and named active acquirers with deal counts.

sources.title: Transition Support Services Critical to Retaining Assets During Advisor Moves
sources.what_it_supports: More than one-third of advisers controlling 41% of assets expected to retire within a decade; independent transitions lose assets and 33% of retiring RIAs expect an external sale.

sources.title: Fee Compression and Rising Service Demands Cause Advisors to Adjust Pricing Structure
sources.what_it_supports: Fee-compression pressure, service expansion, advisory-fee revenue mix, and expected sub-1% pricing for larger client accounts.

sources.title: Asset-based fees remain dominant, fixed and hourly models gaining ground among RIAs
sources.what_it_supports: IAA 2025 Snapshot fee data: 95.5% offer AUM fees, 17.4% use them alone, 78.1% combine them with other arrangements.

sources.title: SEC Adopts Rules and Interpretations to Enhance Protections and Preserve Choice for Retail Investors
sources.what_it_supports: SEC interpretation that investment advisers owe a fiduciary duty across the adviser-client relationship under the Advisers Act.

sources.title: SEC IM Guidance Update: Robo-Advisers
sources.what_it_supports: Automated advice can operate with limited human interaction but remains subject to fiduciary, disclosure, suitability, and compliance obligations.

labor_share.method: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 5239 ($166,457,424,125) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 5239 ($454,212,096,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: ancestor 5239. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

occupations.title: Securities, Commodities, and Financial Services Sales Agents
Executive Secretaries and Executive Administrative Assistants

n_total.source: QCEW 2024 private annual avg establishments (SUSB out of scope for this code; establishments are a proxy for firms — overcounts multi-unit firms)

n_band.rationale: A wealth-RIA bridge uses a 0.88% realized fee proxy [S4] and 29.9% standardized margin for the $500M-$750M Schwab peer group [S3], implying EBITDA/AUM of 0.0088 x 0.299 = 0.0026312 and a $1-10M EBITDA AUM interval of about $380M-$3.8B. IAA reports 16,544 SEC advisers, 67.4% below $1B and 87.3% below $5B [S2]. Log-interpolating within the broad sub-$1B and $1-$5B bands implies roughly 39% in the AUM interval, then a 30% haircut for institutional/private-fund managers, lower realized fees, and non-wealth models yields about 4,500 firms. The 16,575 state advisers [S5] are excluded because their aggregate AUM indicates that most fall below the inferred EBITDA threshold.
caveats: AUM bands are broad and log interpolation is an assumption, not an observed firm count.
The 0.88% fee is a client-account benchmark, not firmwide realized revenue/AUM, and the 29.9% margin is from 2019.
523940 includes institutional asset managers and private-fund advisers whose fee and margin economics differ from wealth RIAs.
State-registered advisers are excluded from the selected count; a small number could nevertheless reach the EBITDA band.

ai_replaceable_share.rationale: Role-level judgments assign high fractions to clerical, client-service, administrative, accounting, and research-production tasks, while retaining human fractions for relationships, business development, supervisory judgment, fiduciary accountability, and complex advice. The 28.66% residual receives a 45% blended fraction. Python weighting yields about 41.6% of wage tasks.
caveats: The three-digit 523 role mix includes brokerage and large asset-manager occupations and is low quality for exact NAICS 523940.
AI-use evidence does not directly measure headcount removal, and fiduciary/compliance review remains.

breakdown_by_role.rationale: Plan drafts, meeting preparation, notes, portfolio explanation, and routine follow-up automate; trust, judgment, coaching, and rainmaking persist.

breakdown_by_role.role: Securities, Commodities, and Financial Services Sales Agents
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Prospecting support, proposals, paperwork, and routine communications automate, while relationship acquisition and regulated selling remain human.
Screening, research synthesis, model maintenance, attribution, and recurring reporting are highly tractable; allocation accountability and novel judgment persist.
Billing, management reporting, reconciliations, controls evidence, and workflow oversight automate, while supervisory and financial accountability remain.
Operational reporting and coordination automate, but people leadership, client escalation, strategy, and vendor/accountability decisions persist.
Data entry, document handling, CRM maintenance, scheduling, and routine correspondence are strongly automatable.
Routine status, scheduling, forms, and account-service communication automate; sensitive money movement and escalations retain human review.
Reconciliation, fee calculation, reporting, and workpaper preparation automate; review, judgment, and attestation remain.
Code generation, testing support, and documentation automate, while architecture, security, integration, and production accountability persist.

breakdown_by_role.role: Executive Secretaries and Executive Administrative Assistants
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Scheduling, meeting preparation, correspondence, travel, and document workflows automate substantially; confidential coordination and judgment remain.

Residual mixes portfolio managers, compliance staff, client-service associates, operations specialists, marketing, legal, and other roles; high workflow exposure is moderated by judgment and accountability.

pi_dist.rationale: AUM fees do not mechanically fall when hours fall, allowing substantial initial retention; fee compression, broader service bundles, and fixed/subscription alternatives return part of the gain to clients.
caveats: Fee structure varies sharply between wealth advice, institutional portfolio management, and private funds inside the code.

pi_moat.rationale: Trust, client history, niche expertise, integrated tax/estate coordination, and fiduciary accountability provide a moderate moat, offset by portable adviser relationships, transition attrition, competitive acquirers, and commoditized allocation.
caveats: Regulatory duty supports accountable service but does not require a human-only delivery model and is not treated as a complete moat.

t50_years.rationale: With 68% reporting some AI use but only an estimated 40% at economically effective deployment, embedded tools and strong workflow fit should carry the industry through 50% within about two years.
caveats: Schwab participants are custody clients and any-use is broader than effective adoption; compliance restrictions can slow deployment.

current_adoption_pct.rationale: The 68% any-use finding is discounted for experimentation, personal tools, and low-intensity applications to estimate effective adoption in material operating workflows.
caveats: No public exact-NAICS measure of scaled AI workflow adoption or labor removal was found.

historical_analogs.value: Robo-advisers and model-portfolio/TAMP automation commoditized allocation and rebalancing while preserving a regulated adviser market; generative AI extends that analogue into planning, communication, research, and operations.
historical_analogs.rationale: The SEC documented algorithmic advisers with little human interaction years before generative AI, while the current adviser population and client count reached records; this bounds both disruption and resilience.
historical_analogs.plausible_range: Workflow augmentation dominant near term; direct mass-affluent substitution plausible over a longer horizon
caveats: Robo-adviser history predates conversational AI and may understate substitution by more capable systems.

owners_60plus_pct.rationale: More than one-third of advisers are expected to retire in the next decade; mapping that horizon to current age and allowing for earlier/later planned retirement yields an estimated one-quarter age 60+.
caveats: Evidence is for advisers across channels, not owners of exact-code firms, and retirement horizon is not an age-60 cross-tab.

succession_shortage_documented.rationale: Cerulli documents more than one-third retiring within a decade and external-sale expectations rising to 33% for independent RIAs, together with asset losses during transitions; this is direct evidence of a material succession-transfer challenge.
caveats: A transition challenge does not mean every retiring practice lacks a successor or will sell.

active_consolidators.rationale: ECHELON's 2025 table lists eight named strategic RIA buyers with at least ten deals each: Wealth Enhancement, Mercer, Merit, Carson, Beacon Pointe, Mariner, Creative Planning, and EP Wealth.
caveats: Eight is a conservative documented subset, not the full buyer universe.

buy_mult.rationale: Advisor Growth Strategies reports an 11.6x median adjusted EBITDA multiple across 60 actual 2025 RIA transactions; the average seller was about $500M AUM, near the inferred lower end of this packet's EBITDA band.
caveats: Median deal value is not a size-tier quote for every $1-10M EBITDA target; adjusted EBITDA, equity, earnouts, and retention payments can inflate headline comparability.

exit_mult.rationale: A professionally managed, faster-growing and less founder-dependent platform can command a premium to the 11.6x current median. The selected 13.0x is modest relative to the 2025 Deal Room's warning that the biggest platforms can attract 20x-plus adjusted EBITDA, avoiding those mega-platform prints as a direct comp.
caveats: Exit premium is not directly observed for a defined $1-10M EBITDA cohort and should not be underwritten without quality improvement; market rates and structure may overwhelm it.

pricing_structure.value: AUM-based recurring fees dominate, usually combined with fixed, hourly, planning, performance, or subscription fees; larger accounts face tiered fee compression.
pricing_structure.rationale: IAA-derived data show 95.5% offer AUM fees but most combine them with other arrangements, while Cerulli documents account-size fee compression and expanding service expectations.
pricing_structure.plausible_range: AUM-led hybrid pricing for wealth RIAs; performance and institutional mandates more important in asset-management subsegments
caveats: Fee-offering incidence is not revenue-weighted and exact-code subsegments differ.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: A roughly 42% task share and an estimated 40% current effective adoption are consistent with rapid operating change; majority tool use today does not imply majority realization of the full labor-saving opportunity.

terminal_value.justification: Human fiduciary advice, trust, coordination, and complex judgment retain paid demand, but automated registered advisers already exist and mature conversational systems can commoditize mass-affluent planning and investment management.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: No-proxy is not confirmed: Form ADV industry counts and SEC duties are direct, while the EBITDA-band count, owner age, effective adoption, role mix, fee realization, and margin bridge use disclosed proxies.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: AUM fees initially retain labor savings, but ongoing fee compression, account-size breakpoints, fixed/subscription alternatives, and client questions about AI-produced work can transfer a meaningful share of value to clients.

dataset_mismatch.description: Labor share is ancestor 5239 with mixed vintages; role mix is three-digit 523 and contains brokerage/large-manager roles; n_total is establishments; n_band is missing and replaced by a low-confidence SEC-RIA AUM/fee/margin estimate.
sensitivity: A wealth-RIA-specific role mix could lower brokerage sales and software weights while raising unlisted client-service operations.
Lower realized fee rates or margins increase the AUM required for the EBITDA band and reduce n_band.
Including qualifying state advisers raises n_band modestly; including institutional managers without correcting their fee economics could overstate it.

market_boundary.selection_basis: Wealth RIAs dominate the fragmented firm-count opportunity and align with the adoption, fee, succession, and M&A evidence; institutional and alternative managers are treated as material code heterogeneity.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: Moves adoption score from 2.5 to 10.0 within the stated range.
Materially changes buyability; the selected value is a disclosed AUM-band interpolation rather than an observed count.
Changes raw labor-value opportunity by about seven percentage points of revenue across the range.
The multiple score ranges from zero to modest, and can reverse if quality or market conditions deteriorate.

reviewer_note: Scrutinize the n_band log interpolation and haircut, the conversion of retirement horizon to owners age 60+, and any use of current adjusted-EBITDA headline multiples where transaction structure differs.

A.arithmetic: Python v3.1.2: A = 10 when t50=0 else min(10,10/t50); t50=2, A=5
